title: Elionurus muticus
taxonomy: Elionurus muticus is classified as a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It belongs to the tribe Andropogoneae and the genus Elionurus. Elionurus muticus is native to tropical and subtropical regions, including parts of Asia and Australia. Its classification also places it within the subfamily Panicoideae. This classification reflects the plant's unique characteristics and evolutionary history within the grass family.

intro: Elionurus muticus, commonly known as wild cinnamon or Chinese silvergrass, is a perennial grass species native to China and Southeast Asia. This plant has been used in traditional medicine for centuries, offering various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which can help to alleviate symptoms of respiratory infections, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Elionurus muticus include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, Elionurus muticus is often used to make herbal preparations, such as decoctions, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including coughs, colds, and digestive problems.
